mentions.note: Don't sell Apple — the Siri/AI gap is real but history says they eventually deliver a superior consumer product.
Penalized for borrowing to build data centers it may not monetize; suffers unfairly from comparison to peers.
Cited as the AI monetization benchmark Amazon's data center spend is measured against.
Autonomous driving company; Cramer will not render verdict until interviewing co-founder/Waymo CEO Chris Merson about FedEx Freight autonomous route successes and safety metrics.
Runs autonomous routes for FedEx Freight; Cramer refuses to render a verdict until he gets co-founder Chris Urmson on the show.
Mentioned as the partner producing Meta's new custom chip — the report that triggered Meta's morning selloff.
Cited as proof the AI boom is hardware-intensive — a heavy-equipment name turned big AI winner via power plant and data center construction.
Terrific company with excellent chart; buy here and on weakness; one of Philadelphia's best.
Terrific company with a great chart — buy some here and add more if it comes down.
Caller pick: Walgreens and Rite Aid have been cleared from the field, Aetna is crushing it, and the only real competitor left is Amazon.
CEO David Joyner navigated competitive scrum; Walgreens largely gone, Amazon lacks insurance arm; CVS-Aetna synergy crushing it; strong buy.
Rallied with the data center supply complex on intense demand and insufficient supply.
Most likely single winner of the AI race with Gemini as default on Apple's 2.5B-device base; market is ignoring YouTube's extraordinary profitability and Waymo.
Dominant HBM supplier at 56.4% share and cheap at 7x current-year earnings, but memory is boom-bust; take a medium-sized position and leave room to buy weakness.
Caller pick: inexpensive with Arvind Krishna executing well; buy some now and add into the periodic panic selloffs.
Cited as the simpler, cleaner beverage comparison versus PepsiCo's complicated snack-plus-beverage structure.
Among the data-center-supply names leading the S&P; benefits from acute shortage but carries commodity-cycle risk.
Founder using AI to disrupt insurance, impressive customer growth but company not yet profitable; would change positive view if starts making money.
Cramer's preferred Marriott: a long-term winner with much better long-term growth than the timeshare business.
Long-term winner despite up 70% YTD and down from 2021 highs; prefer over Marriott Vacations (VAC); much better longer-term growth.
Initial panic on Broadcom chip production and capex ramp reversed to a $28 gain; Zuckerberg likely has a high-ROI monetization plan across 3.5B users and WhatsApp.
Copilot seen as a suboptimal AI product, but Cramer speculates there may be an undisclosed product that changes the narrative.
Ripped higher with the storage complex on memory pricing, but Cramer prefers proprietary Nvidia to commodity memory names near the top of a boom cycle.
Senior housing operator with a reliable pattern of institutional buying at the 50-day moving average; wait for the next shallow pullback for an excellent entry.

Senior living operator with reliable 50-day moving average support pattern; seven similar buy-on-weakness cycles past year; currently moving higher, shallow pullback expected to offer next entry point; solid technical setup.
Most proprietary chip company in history and also on allocation; traders' reflex to sell it when commodity memory rallies is misguided given commodity names now carry higher forward multiples.

Frito-Lay North America volumes turned negative as gasoline inflation squeezed consumer snack budgets; Cramer fears the start of a slow pricing rollback and says only drastic price cuts can reverse the domestic dive.
Small-cap grid and data center hardware maker with a 24% sequential earnings beat; pullback from $400+ to the $340s offers a good entry at 35x earnings on a ~$13/share 2028 estimate.

Senior living operator; broke above $37 resistance recently on high volume, crossed $40; bull-pattern base after 40% pullback from $36 peak; Fitzpatrick projects $55 by year-end; embracing AI to improve employee efficiency.
Broke out above $37 resistance and crossed $40 today on high volume after a 20-month rounded base; Fitzpatrick and Cramer see $55 before year-end.
Cramer wishes they made money; won't endorse until profitability arrives, though he credits the caller's presentation.

Dominates HBM market (56% share); revenue tripled, earnings swung to $30B profit (2024) and $153B projected (2026); trades at 7x earnings despite strength; supply-demand tight through 2027-28. Put on medium position, leave room to buy weakness; volatility expected from $26.4B IPO.
Analyst raised target from $1,200 to $2,000 on further memory price increases, but Cramer objects that a commodity chipmaker now trades at a higher forward multiple than Nvidia.
Referenced as the $75B IPO that preceded the SK Hynix ADR offering, part of the market's recent test of appetite for mega equity issuance.
Services counterpart to Preformed in the AI infrastructure stack; up more than 200% over 12 months and still a good place to be.
Part of the data-storage complex topping the S&P leaderboard on tight supply; commodity exposure makes it vulnerable when the cycle turns.
Caller's actual holding, up ~70% year to date; Cramer likes it but says it's not as good as Marriott hotels.
Up 70% YTD from 2021 lows but Cramer prefers MAR (Marriott Hotels) for superior long-term growth profile.
Rallied with storage peers on data center demand and pricing, but Cramer would rather own the hyperscalers than commodity storage.
Senior housing REIT in a clean three-year uptrend with institutional buying at the 40-week moving average; buy here and leave room to add on pullbacks.

Largest senior housing REIT in 3-year uptrend; 40-week moving average shows strong institutional buying on dips; demographic tailwind (80+ population +30% by 2030) drives secular growth; buy on weakness, room to run higher.
Announced Monday price rollbacks specifically naming Frito-Lay chips and Pepsi sodas — likely forced PepsiCo's pricing hand.
Rate-sensitive timber REIT with a ~3.7% yield; Cramer is more bullish than the Street but says you only buy it if you believe rates ultimately go lower.
